blocks.text: When a private company sells stock to the public for the first time, it runs an initial public offering (IPO) under the Securities Act of 1933 — the 'paper act' built on one idea: full and fair disclosure. The company (the issuer) hires investment banks (underwriters) and files a registration statement with the SEC.

blocks.definition: The disclosure document filed with the SEC to register a public offering. It describes the business, its finances, management, risk factors, and how proceeds will be used. Part of it becomes the prospectus — the disclosure document that must be delivered to every buyer of the new issue.

blocks.text: The three periods of registration

Registration unfolds in three periods. Pre-filing: no offers, no sales, no publicity about the deal — silence (an illegal early promotion is called 'gun jumping'). Cooling-off period (a minimum of roughly 20 days while the SEC reviews): the underwriters may distribute a preliminary prospectus (red herring), publish a bare-bones tombstone ad, and take non-binding indications of interest — but no sales, no money accepted, and no research reports or sales literature about the issue. Post-effective: the final prospectus exists, the price is set, and sales can happen.

blocks.definition: The preliminary prospectus used during the cooling-off period. It has no final price and no effective date, and it carries a red-ink warning that the registration hasn't yet become effective. It gauges interest — it can't be used to sell.

blocks.caption: What's allowed when
rows: Red herring, tombstone, indications of interest, due diligence meeting
Sales, accepting funds, sending research or sales literature
Altering the disclosure; claiming SEC approval

blocks.text: Near the end of the cooling-off period the underwriters hold a due diligence meeting — a final scrub of the disclosure among the issuer, underwriters, lawyers, and accountants — then the deal is priced and the registration goes effective. If the SEC finds the filing incomplete, it issues a deficiency letter, which stops the clock until the issuer cures the problem.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: During BrightLeaf's cooling-off period, a rep collects a customer's indication of interest for 500 shares at the expected $15-$17 range. That indication is not binding on either side. Only after the registration goes effective at $16 can the customer actually buy — and she must receive the final prospectus no later than with the confirmation.

An indication of interest is binding on NO ONE. And a tombstone ad is not an offer to sell — it's the only advertising permitted during cooling-off, limited to bare facts: issuer name, security, price range, and who to call for a prospectus.
Aftermarket rules

Prospectus delivery doesn't stop at the offering: dealers must keep delivering the prospectus to aftermarket buyers for 25 days after the effective date when the stock lists on an exchange or Nasdaq (90 days for a non-listed IPO, 40 days for a non-listed follow-on). And under FINRA Rule 5130, industry insiders — member firms, their employees, and immediate family they support — are 'restricted persons' who may not buy shares of a common-stock IPO at the offering price.

Remember from Chapter 1: SEC effectiveness is not approval. The final prospectus can never claim the SEC approved or verified anything — that misstatement is itself fraud. Every prospectus carries a disclaimer saying exactly this.

Public companies keep raising capital after the IPO. A follow-on (additional issue) offering sells NEW shares — primary, proceeds to the issuer, dilutes existing holders. A registered secondary distribution sells EXISTING shares held by insiders or early investors — the sellers get the money, share count unchanged. A combined (split) offering mixes both in one deal.

blocks.definition: Lets a seasoned issuer register a large block of securities once, then sell pieces 'off the shelf' over up to three years (two for some issuers) as market conditions allow — no new registration each time. Each takedown just needs a prospectus supplement.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: Meridian Utilities shelf-registers $2 billion of debt. Eight months later rates dip, and it sells $500 million of bonds off the shelf within days — a speed impossible if it had to start a fresh 20-day registration review.

Well-known seasoned issuers (WKSIs — very large, established filers) get the most flexibility: their shelf registrations become effective automatically on filing. That's the reward for years of continuous 1934 Act reporting: the market already has the disclosure.
Dilution and preemptive rights

Dilution is why shareholders care about follow-ons: issuing new shares shrinks each existing holder's ownership percentage and spreads earnings across more shares (lower EPS). That's also why common shareholders often hold preemptive rights (Chapter 5) — the right to buy new shares before the public and maintain their proportional stake, delivered mechanically through a rights offering.

blocks.title: Example — dilution math
blocks.text: You own 100,000 of 10 million shares — 1%. The company issues 2 million new shares in a follow-on you skip. You now own 100,000 of 12 million: 0.83%. With preemptive rights you'd have been offered 20,000 of the new shares (1% of 2 million) first, letting you stay at 1%.

New shares + issuer gets proceeds = primary (even for a 50-year-old company). Existing shares + selling shareholders get proceeds = secondary. A 'combined' or 'split' offering is both at once. Shelf registration = registered once, sold over up to three years.

Not every offering registers with the SEC. The 1933 Act exempts certain SECURITIES outright and certain TRANSACTIONS, where the security itself isn't special but the way it's sold avoids a public offering. Keep the two lists separate — it's a favorite exam distinction.

items: Exempt SECURITIES (never register): U.S. government and agency issues, municipal bonds, bank securities, short-term commercial paper and bankers' acceptances (270 days or less), securities of nonprofits, and insurance policies/fixed annuities (variable products are NOT exempt).
Exempt TRANSACTIONS (the sale avoids registration): Reg D private placements, Rule 147 intrastate offerings, Reg A+ small offerings, Reg S offshore sales, Rule 144A sales to QIBs.

blocks.definition: A transaction exemption for securities sold privately rather than publicly. Under Rule 506(b): unlimited accredited investors plus up to 35 non-accredited (but sophisticated) purchasers, no general solicitation or advertising. Rule 506(c) allows advertising — but then EVERY buyer must be accredited, and the issuer must take reasonable steps to verify it.

An individual with income over $200,000 ($300,000 joint) in each of the last two years with the same expectation this year, or net worth over $1 million EXCLUDING the primary residence — plus institutions, banks, insurance companies, and holders of certain licenses (Series 7, 65, 82).

blocks.text: Private placements typically deliver disclosure through a private placement memorandum (PPM) rather than a prospectus. Investors usually sign a subscription agreement and may be asked to attest to their accredited status. The securities they receive are 'restricted' — often stamped with a restrictive legend — and illiquid until an exemption for resale applies.
Rule 144 — the exit door

Private-placement stock is restricted: bought unregistered, it can't be freely resold. Rule 144 is the exit door. Non-affiliates must hold restricted stock of a reporting company six months, then sell freely (12 months for non-reporting issuers). Affiliates (officers, directors, 10%+ owners) face volume limits every time they sell control stock: in any 90 days, no more than the GREATER of 1% of shares outstanding or the average weekly trading volume of the prior four weeks — and they file Form 144 at or before the sale.

blocks.title: Example — Rule 144 volume math
blocks.text: A director wants to sell shares of a company with 10 million shares outstanding. The last four weeks traded 120,000 / 100,000 / 90,000 / 90,000 shares — an average of 100,000. One percent of outstanding is 100,000, so the greater-of test allows 100,000 shares in the next 90 days. If average weekly volume had been 140,000, she could sell 140,000.

Keep the players straight: Reg D governs the private SALE; Rule 144 governs the RESALE. Restricted stock = how you acquired it (unregistered — holding period applies). Control stock = who you are (affiliate — volume limits apply). An affiliate's registered shares face volume limits but no holding period; a non-affiliate's restricted shares face a holding period but, after it, no limits.

Other exempt transactions worth a glance: Rule 147/147A intrastate offerings (issuer doing business and all buyers in one state — resales to out-of-state buyers barred for six months), Regulation A+ small offerings (Tier 1 up to $20 million, Tier 2 up to $75 million, with a lighter 'offering circular' disclosure), Regulation S offshore sales to non-U.S. persons, and Rule 144A resales of unregistered securities among qualified institutional buyers (QIBs, $100 million+ under management).

Underwriting is how the deal actually gets sold. The issuer picks a lead (managing) underwriter, which forms a syndicate of firms that sign the agreement among underwriters, share the risk, and split the selling. A selling group of additional firms may help distribute shares without capital at risk. The lead runs the book, allocates shares, and stabilizes the aftermarket if needed.

blocks.definition: The syndicate BUYS the entire issue from the issuer and resells it to the public — acting as principal. Unsold shares are the underwriters' problem. The issuer is guaranteed its money. This is the standard structure for large corporate deals.

The underwriter acts as the issuer's AGENT, selling what it can with no obligation to buy the rest. Variants: all-or-none (cancel unless every share sells) and mini-max (cancel unless a stated minimum sells). Investor funds sit in escrow until the contingency is met — if it fails, money goes back.

blocks.caption: Underwriting commitments
rows: Issuer — deal cancels if minimum unmet

blocks.text: The spread and its pieces

The underwriting spread is the difference between what the public pays (the public offering price, or POP) and what the issuer receives. It splits three ways: the manager's fee (to the lead, the smallest piece per share but earned on every share), the underwriting fee (to syndicate members for capital risk), and the selling concession — the largest piece — to whoever actually sells the shares. A selling-group member earns only the concession; a reallowance is the small slice available to non-syndicate FINRA members who sell.

blocks.title: Example — the spread
blocks.text: POP $20.00, issuer receives $18.80: a $1.20 spread. Roughly, the manager might take $0.20, the underwriting fee $0.30, and the selling concession $0.70. A selling-group member earns only the $0.70 concession — it took no capital risk. Note the buyer pays no commission on a new issue; compensation is inside the POP.

Municipal underwriting adds one vocabulary pair: negotiated (issuer picks the underwriter and negotiates terms — typical for revenue bonds) versus competitive (issuer publishes an official notice of sale and syndicates submit sealed bids; lowest interest cost wins — typical for GO bonds). Corporate deals are almost always negotiated.

Firm commitment = principal = underwriter risk. Best efforts = agent = issuer risk. If the question mentions escrowed customer funds, it's an all-or-none or mini-max deal. And IPO buyers pay the POP with no added commission — the spread is the underwriters' pay.
